citations: Elasticity of taxable income literature (Saez, Slemrod, Giertz)

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earner substitution elasticity for middle-income decile 4 reflects moderate responsiveness to wage changes. citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on elasticity of taxable income
Keane and Rogerson (2012) on labor supply elasticities across wage levels

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earner substitution elasticity for decile 4 (lower-middle earnings) is likely moderate. Empirical labor supply literature suggests substitution elasticities are heterogeneous and generally lower for lower earners. Decile 4 is below median earnings, so I expect moderate responsiveness to wage changes—higher than very low earners but likely lower than high-wage workers who face higher marginal tax rates. The 0.25 central estimate reflects typical findings for mid-to-lower income workers, with substantial uncertainty reflected in the 0.05–0.65 range spanning conservative and more elastic estimates.

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the 4th decile of earnings distribution, I estimate a modest substitution elasticity reflecting moderate wage sensitivity. Mid-income primary earners typically face fewer constraints than lower-decile workers but show less responsiveness than higher earners. The range 0.05–0.65 reflects uncertainty around whether this group exhibits behavioral response closer to lower-income estimates (~0.1) or approaches higher estimates seen in some structural models (~0.4–0.6). citations: Keane and Moffitt (2005) on labor supply elasticities

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the fourth earnings decile (moderate-income workers), substitution elasticity is expected to be modest but positive. Empirical literature generally finds elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.5 for primary earners at middle-income levels, with lower-income workers sometimes showing somewhat higher responses. The fourth decile represents workers with material income but not high earnings, for whom both income and substitution effects matter. I estimate a central tendency around 0.25 with considerable uncertainty, ranging from near-zero to moderately substantial responses.
